transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
How are you?
Hi, Mansi.
I'm great.
How are you doing?
I'm doing good.
Good.
Excuse me.
I'm getting—I have a bit of a cold, so.
You sound a little like a frog.
Is it cold, eh?
Yeah, we actually—a lot of the eastern part of the U.S.
got a huge storm last—over the weekend and early this week.
So we got about two feet of snow here, which is a lot.
Which is a lot.
I hope you are safe.
You know, I'm in Vermont, which is near the Canadian—
So we are kind of used to having snow.
I think it was harder in places where they kind of weren't used to that much snow and things really shut down.
So luckily we're okay here, but thank you.
And I wasn't sure, Mansi, who is attending today.
So I'm happy to wait for a few minutes if you think more people will be here.
I'm not sure if Ankit's coming or.
Yeah, I think Ankit is coming.
And then we have Karthi joining.
Karthi is the one that is handling the publishing of all CVA articles.
And then I'll let Karthi introduce herself.
Hi, nice to meet you.
Hi.
Hi, Bailey.
This is Karthi.
Nice to meet you.
Yeah.
I'm working as an SEO, Senior SEO Specialist here.
Yeah, I have like 10 posts here for experience.
I'm here at S1 for the past 11 months.
Yeah, okay.
Well, welcome.
Feel free to reach out to me if you ever have any questions or issues with the CVE pages.
I know it's a lot of content.
Yeah, it's very, sure.
It's very nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you as well.
Hey, yeah, I wanted you to meet Karthi as she's the one in the background helping publish all the CVE pages and then indexation, all those things.
So I think it's best to have her on these calls as well.
That's the reason I had it.
And she can have updates going forward for CVE side of pages on both the publishing side and...
No, that's great.
It's really nice to meet you.
And I'm happy to, yeah, stay in touch.
And I think if you're not on our Slack channel already, I can make sure that you're at it as well, just so you can reach out directly in case you ever have questions about anything.
No, Ankit has added me.
I'm part of the Slack channel.
Perfect.
That's great.
All right.
Well, let me jump in to this week.
So we had a great, I think, really productive kind of overview of your goals for this coming year, last week.
So I just wanted to kind of let you know the next steps of what's coming out from that.
So usually what we do is I sort of take the learnings from that conversation and take it back to our director's strategy team.
So talk to them a little bit about your goals for the upcoming year.
think in this.

case, the focus on conversion, leaning into differentiation, and what we're going to do, what we're doing now from that is sort of just taking a look at the strategy, looking at what's been working so far, put that against your goals for this year, and make some recommendations of places where we might want to lean in or shift or experiment a little bit more when it comes to AI visibility.
So we're going to take that, and then we're going to put together a report with some recommendations, and get that to you by next week.
Well, that sounds good.

So while we're doing it, can we also look at a few matrices that we need to change to, I mean, not specifically for GrowthX team, but how the teams here at S1 should look at, and in terms of what matrices matter now.
I'd say to the last year, I see traffic as a declining metric because there's a lot of adoption for zero-click searches and then the shift in AI search with AI search.
So, what metrics do really matter, say for a team like the search team, of course, the North Star is conversion and we want to shift to report on conversion rates.
Here, conversion would mean website conversions, form fields and things that we have on the web, and then maybe classifying them from back to, say, conversions from organic acquisition.
That would include both traditional organic search and then also AI systems that send leads our way.
Yeah, I'm just trying to say that also suggest some matrices that you think would be right to keep for the year 26 so that we don't end up choosing a declining metric.
Yeah.
Because right now, the space is changing very dynamically.
And for example, about AI citations.
So AI citations, I see that as a declining metric because early when ChatGPT, you know, was picking up, they were citing other websites a lot also during their training period and a crawling period.
Now for informational queries, see citations way lesser than compared to what we were looking at six months back.
So for a change like this, and if say citations.
It was in our KPIs.
So even if we do work, we do not do work, that's a change in system, right?
So we can't really control and we do not want to pick a metric like that that is controllable, say, by an algorithm or an LLM system that makes our jobs even harder.
So I'm just wanting to be cautious here of what we choose and pick.
And what matters is we start to report on in the coming year.
I think that makes 100% sense.
And, you know, we can take a look at, you know, what we recommend to measure success based on those goals.
And I think you're right.
And with the big shifts, it can be hard to figure out exactly what.
I mean, even with citations and AI visibility, you know, that's it's attribution is hard, like we talked about, because, you know, the user behavior is often they look something up.
And then they go Google it, right?
They don't always come in through.
So sometimes it can be hard to tie those things together.
So I think also making a recommendation of some, you know, different metrics, I think, like a broader recommendation too, I think would work as well.
So I will definitely flag that for the team and bring you some recommendations kind of based on that.
All right.
Sounds good.
Okay.
In terms of CVE updates, we're on schedule publishing as the release plan stated.
And we fixed the trailing slash and also updated the pages.
And I went and checked yesterday and it looked like almost everything had indexed.
I think it was only like 40 or 50 had not.
So it's looking, it's good now.
We'll keep an eye on it, but it looks like that fixed the issue.
Yep.
Even a trailing, we're keeping the tie.
and Karthip was helping track all of it.
So I think we are in good shape now.
Yeah.
I also wanted to let you know, your Looker, we've added the CVE pages there.
So just to get a better overview of tracking in terms of impressions and clicks, just to get a better visual overview.
So there's now a page there where you'll be able to see that.
And I'll continue to kind of report on that along with the other metrics too, so we can keep an eye on it.
Wonderful.
In terms of CS101, we have four new articles coming today.
I did want to ask you, I think, I'm not sure if it was the week where Mansi was out, but I wanted to check in and make sure, see if you had any additional feedback on last week's articles.
I think they went right to the next round of review.
So I just wanted to check in and make sure there was nothing else you wanted from us, Mansi, on those.
No, those got...
Articles for good to go, so we directly, I and Mahindra checked on those, and then we passed it for PMM review.
For this week's article, we have some feedback, and I'll be sending you after this call, probably.
Okay, perfect.
Thanks so much.
And was there anything else?
I think that was all I had.
just wanted to check in about those articles.
Otherwise, I think we're good there.
I think in terms of just a reporting snapshot, know we sort of dug into more of a retrospective, and I'll do a full January reporting.
I'll deliver that for our next meeting, but nothing of concern here.
You know, engagement rates trending up across the pillars.
You know, the LLM, the referral traffic is still steady.
The impressions for CVE are also trending up, so all kind of directionally.
time.
Bye.
Bye.
Positive, I would say.
And I'll dig more into reporting for next week.

So when I have the full month of data, is there anything in particular sort of you would, my plan is just to generally report across the metrics and kind of what we're seeing, but if there's anything in particular you had questions about, I can make sure to answer those or look into those when we do our monthly reporting.
So just let me know.
All right.
So can we together look once at the release plan for CV in the document that we had?
Let me just pull that up.
All right.
All right.
All right.
you.
So, I'll check in with our editorial team, but I believe yesterday this should have published on schedule.
Oh, yes, she did.
She updated the number.
So, here's where we are for this week.
Okay, got it.
So, we are following this release plan, date to date, and say by, so we are done for the year 2025, all CVs are published.
Yeah, and I think 2024 should be complete now, too, as of yesterday.
Okay.
So, I can check in to make sure that's the case, but I asked our editorial team to update this document after they publish, so we have a real number and that we know we're kind of on our timeline.
Okay.
So on average, for the newer CVs, how many CVs do you think comes each month?
Let's see.
For the newer ones, it looks like we've been publishing – it changes a bit week to week, so I'd have to go in and I think run the numbers.
But it looks like we've been publishing for the new ones somewhere between – usually it looks like around 1,500.
I'm not sure why that week was late.
I'll have to ask about that.
But it looks like we've been doing around 1,500 a week for the new ones.
Okay, got it.
Yeah, that's like quite a number.
So, I mean, a good number, a big number.
So, I'm just thinking how we'd want to approach this.
Also, if you could, I think in a month or more, we'll exactly know.
On an average per week, what to expect for newer CVs, we'll know of a trend.

Also, would you, so one thing that we have been brainstorming about the vulnerability database, and I wanted to leave you with a question, or the same question we've had, is, do we want to localize this in other languages?
So, this type of content, and then, what would be the implications of doing that?
Would it turn out good for us, not too good for us?

Another thought is, would we end up in a bloated situation of content, say, if we end up building a database of 10,000 pages in a month or more, that I feel that we will be at that stage, and then having that in 7 other languages is 70,000 pages.
And if we continue to add, say, 6,000.
CVs each month on keeping this average of 1,500 per week.
So are we going to make this site the biggest site in the world or not?
mean, things like that.
So I'm just thinking out loud and I'm wanting an opinion here of what you think about translating these in other languages.
That's a good question.
And let me take that back to our technical SEO team because I think they'll have a better sense of, A, I think what the technical implications are for, you know, publishing that scale of content.
And if there are other kind of downstream effects that we might not be thinking through, I feel like that would be good for them to flag any questions or concerns they might have.
In terms of, like, traffic from those other locations, off the top of my head, like, what are, I guess, what would be.
For localization, what would be the priorities?
Because, I mean, I think, too, if you wanted to roll it out, you know, you could do it as a scaled rollout as well.
What would be the priorities for those other kind of target languages, target markets?
Yeah, so we'd have seven of those that currently sits on the website.
So we have been localizing other places of the website.
We do have the priority order, which ones to do first and then the next ones.
But the question more is if we want to do it and if this particular content type holds value in localization or not.
So I have done my homework, some research and some opinion already in place.
But I'm just wanting to not influence Grotech's opinion.
So I'm just wanting to hear from you what you'd suggest and then we could collectively decide on what to do.
All right, that sounds good.
We'll give you an independent, unbiased opinion.
Okay, that sounds good.
I'll take that to our team today, and then hopefully we'll have an answer for you on that next time we talk.
All right.
Sounds good.
I think looking at our agenda, I think the only other thing was the AI visibility tool that I told you about.
We now have an official launch, and it'll be rolling out from February 2nd to 7th.
So at that point, we'll be able to get your team logged in, and I'll be able to do a demo kind of walking you through it.
So you can take a look at it.
All right.
Sounds good.
Yeah, I was always happy to share feedback on newer things in the space.
Yeah, that would be great.
Yeah, it's a work in progress, and we want it to be kind of as useful as possible, both for us and to give, you know, your team access to that visibility.
So we always love feedback there.
That'd be great.
Sounds good.
All right.
I think that is it from me.
Was there anything else you wanted to touch base about today?
No, I'm good as well.
Okay.
Thanks.
I'll send a follow up right after this, just so we have a summary.
All right.
Thanks everyone.
Thank you.
Bye.
Thank you.
Bye.
Thank you.
